Leaving Flatland

Part four: a sculpture that exists nowhere. No triangles, no vertices, no file — one function answers "how far is the surface?" and a camera full of rays does the rest. Drag to walk around it.

Scroll down to read ↓

Three parts of this series have leaned on one workhorse: the distance field. It was always a texture — a grid where every pixel memorized its distance to the nearest surface, rebuilt every frame by nine jump-flood passes. Adding a third dimension to that plan multiplies every texture by a third axis and the budget collapses. So this part keeps the idea and discards the storage. A distance field is just a function of position; nothing says it has to be sampled and remembered rather than evaluated on demand. For shapes made of mathematics — spheres, boxes, tori — the distance is a one-line formula, exact to machine precision, in any number of dimensions: 

fn sdSphere(p, r)  =  length(p) − r        // negative inside, zero on the skin, positive outside
fn sdTorus(p, t)   =  length(vec2(length(p.xz) − t.x, p.y)) − t.y

The whole scene becomes one function, map(p): the minimum of every shape's distance, because the nearest surface of a world is the nearest surface of its nearest thing. The renderings on this page contain no geometry whatsoever — no mesh, no triangles — just map, called about a hundred times per pixel.

Step 1 · Sculptingmin() builds worlds, smooth min() builds creatures

Combining shapes with min() gives the hard weld of machine parts: surfaces intersect in a crease. Replace it with a smooth minimum — a min with a soft elbow — and surfaces within distance k of each other fuse like droplets: 

h = clamp(0.5 + 0.5·(b − a)/k, 0, 1)
smin = mix(b, a, h) − k·h·(1 − h)   // k is the radius of persuasion

This is the third appearance of "two things merging before they touch" in this series — the metaballs of part one and the wax of the lava lamp were the same idea in texture form. Here it is analytic, and it is the entire craft of SDF modeling: artists build characters from dozens of primitives and one well-chosen k per joint.

Drag k to zero and the three shapes are strangers sharing a floor — every contact a hard crease. Raise it and they begin to negotiate: at 0.2 the orbiting sphere drags a tendril of box along with it; at 0.5 the trio is one creature. Nothing about the shapes changed. Only the definition of "nearest."

Step 2 · Sphere tracingLeaping through certified emptiness

A camera makes one ray per pixel, and each ray must find the first surface in its way. The brute force is to inch forward in fixed steps — thousands of evaluations, and still no guarantee against stepping clean through a thin wall. The distance function offers a better deal, the same one the jump-flood texture offered the cascades in part one: map(p) is a certificate. If the nearest surface is d away, a sphere of radius d around you touches nothing, and you may leap exactly d with perfect safety.

That algorithm is sphere tracing, named by John Hart in 1996. Far from everything it leaps in great bounds; near a surface it brakes, geometrically, to a stop exactly on the skin. Its cost is honest and visible — and worth staring at, because it is the first cost in this series that depends on where you look:

This is the same scene as the hero, colored by how many steps each pixel's ray needed. Faces that meet rays head-on are cheap violet; every silhouette burns white-hot, because rays that graze a surface receive only tiny certificates, step after step. Now drag the budget down: the marcher gives up early, and surfaces erode from their own silhouettes inward — the most expensive pixels are the first to fail. Every production sphere tracer ships with exactly this compromise tuned into it.

Aside · Demoscene provenance

The technique's modern form was honed in the demoscene, where an entire animated world had to fit in four kilobytes — no room for meshes, but plenty for formulas. Íñigo Quílez's catalogue of distance functions and his smooth-minimum note are the de facto textbook, and the reason half the shaders on Shadertoy open with fn map(p).

Step 3 · Light, on a budgetWhat the gradient knows

Shading needs surface normals, and the distance function hands them over too: the gradient of a distance field — which way "farther" points — is exactly the surface normal, sampled with four extra calls to map. But the striking gift is shadows. March a second ray from the surface toward the light: if it reaches the light, lit; if it hits something, shadow. That much is free. The insight is in the near misses: 

penumbra = min(penumbra, k · d / t)   // d: how close the ray came; t: how far along it was

A shadow ray that squeaked past an edge at close range was nearly blocked, so it should be nearly dark. The smallest d/t along the march measures exactly that near-miss angle, and one multiply turns it into a soft penumbra — no area light sampled, no blur pass, just the certificates the march was already collecting, read a second way. Ambient occlusion plays the same game in miniature: probe map a few times along the normal, and wherever the answers come back smaller than the distance probed, something overhangs and the crevice darkens.

Both knobs are lies a photographer would recognize. Shadow sharpness k pretends the light has area (drag it to "overcast" and watch the sculpture sit in a cloudy day); ambient occlusion pretends the sky is an even dome that crevices see less of. Toggle AO to zero and the sculpture's folds go flat and weightless — that grounding you just lost is what the trick buys, for five distance calls.

Step 4 · AssemblyThe garden at your light's mercy

Everything at once, with the light handed to you: move your cursor and the key light orbits with it, dragging penumbras across the pedestal. The debug views are the chapter in two pictures — normals is the gradient field wearing its direction as color, and step count is the cost landscape, silhouettes ablaze.

Notice what is missing, too. The copper blob hangs centimeters above a warm stone pedestal, and the pedestal does not blush — no bounced light, anywhere. The fog in the distance is a blend with the background color, not the scattering medium part three built; the sky lights nothing but the haze. Each of those was solved in flatland, where light genuinely traveled. Here in three dimensions we are back to point lights and clever lies, because honest transport got left behind with the textures.

CodaThe debt comes due

This part bought the third dimension by trading away the thing parts one through three were proudest of. Radiance cascades answered "what does every point see?" — light arrived, bounced, scattered. Sphere tracing answers only "what does the camera see?", and every photons- bouncing question got replaced by a heuristic: shadows from near misses, skylight from a dot product, contact darkness from five probes up the normal. Good lies, fast lies — load-bearing lies.

There is an equation that states exactly what the lies are approximating: the light leaving any point is what it emits plus the integral, over every direction, of the light arriving — the rendering equation, written down by James Kajiya in 1986. Two parts of this series solved it in 2D by brute hierarchy. In 3D there is no such luxury, but there is an older, stranger tool: dice. The next part builds a renderer whose every pixel is a gambling den — and aims it at the most photographed cardboard box in computer science.

Light in a Browser Tab, part four · part one: The Lava Lamp · part two: The Bonfire · part three: The Fog

Standing on the shoulders of: Hart, "Sphere Tracing: A Geometric Method for the Antialiased Ray Tracing of Implicit Surfaces" (1996) · Quílez, "Distance Functions" & "Smooth Minimum" (2008–) · Zhukov, Iones & Kronin, "An Ambient Light Illumination Model" (1998) · Abbott, "Flatland: A Romance of Many Dimensions" (1884)
